content.problemStatement:
**This problem has the exact same setup as qUiRkY qUesTs (Hard). The only difference between them is that in this version, you must solve the problem only for the given array.**

Suppose you have an array BBB. Consider performing the following operation on it:


First, choose any non-empty subsequence of BBB.
Then, replace each element of this subsequence with the MEX†^\dagger† of BBB.

Note that while you choose a subsequence, the replacement is done with the MEX of the entire array BBB, not only the elements of the subsequence.


For example, if B=[1,0,4,1,2]B = [1, 0, 4, 1, 2]B=[1,0,4,1,2] and you choose the subsequence [1,0,1][1, 0, 1][1,0,1], those elements will be replaced by the MEX of BBB (which equals 333), to obtain [3,3,4,3,2][3, 3, 4, 3, 2][3,3,4,3,2].

We define f(B)f(B)f(B) to be the **maximum** possible sum of BBB that can be obtained by performing the given operation any number of times (possibly, zero).

Note that since BBB is changed after a move, its MEX may also change for future moves.

Now, for the actual problem.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN. Compute f(A)f(A)f(A).

†^\dagger† The MEX (minimum excludant) of an array of integers is defined as the smallest non-negative integer which does not occur in the array. For example:


The MEX of [2,2,1][2,2,1][2,2,1] is 000 because 000 is not in the array.
The MEX of [0,1,2][0,1,2][0,1,2] is 333 because 0,1,20, 1, 20,1,2 are present in the array but 333 is not.
The MEX of [3,0,1][3,0,1][3,0,1] is 222 because 0,10, 10,1 are present in the array but 222 is not.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​




Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer on a new line: the value of f(A)f(A)f(A) for the given array AAA.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2×1052\times10^52×105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: It's optimal to not perform any operations, and obtain 15=4+6+515 = 4 + 6 + 515=4+6+5 as the sum.
Test case 222: Consider the following sequence of operations:

First, choose the subsequence [2][2][2] at index 222, and replace it by the MEX of AAA (which is 111).
The array is now [0,1][0, 1][0,1].
Next, choose the entire array as the subsequence, and replace all elements by the MEX of AAA (which is now 222).
The array is now [2,2][2, 2][2,2], with a sum of 444. This is the maximum sum possible.
